questions.question: A component receives price and quantity as props, keeps total in useState, and syncs it with a useEffect calling setTotal(price * quantity). The React docs flag this as their most common code smell — why?
choices: The Effect is missing a cleanup function to cancel the pending setTotal before the next run
Calling a state setter inside an Effect violates the Rules of Hooks, which forbid hooks below the top level
Derived values should be computed during render; mirroring one into state adds an extra render pass and risks total drifting out of sync
total should live in a useRef instead, since refs persist across renders without triggering re-renders
questions.explanation: State that mirrors a computable value creates two sources of truth: the component first renders with the stale total, then the Effect fires and forces a second render — and any code path that updates one side but not the other makes them drift. Anything derivable from props or state should be a plain expression in render (const total = price * quantity); Effects are reserved for synchronizing with external systems, and nothing external is involved here. Cleanup solves stale async results, not derivation; calling a setter in an Effect is legal under the Rules of Hooks; and a ref would hide the value from rendering entirely.

questions.question: Two users vote at the same instant; both requests run choice.votes += 1 then choice.save(), and one vote is lost. Which change removes the race, and why?
choices: Wrap the read and the save in transaction.atomic() so the two requests serialize
Assign choice.votes = F('votes') + 1 before save(), so the increment happens inside the UPDATE statement, in the database
Call save(update_fields=['votes']) so only the votes column is written back
Move the increment into a post_save signal so it runs exactly once per saved instance
questions.explanation: The bug is a read-modify-write race: both requests read votes=N in Python and both write back N+1. F() pushes the arithmetic into SQL — UPDATE ... SET votes = votes + 1 — so the database applies each increment atomically no matter how the requests interleave. transaction.atomic gives all-or-nothing commit, not isolation: both transactions can still read the same starting value. update_fields only narrows which columns are written, still carrying the stale Python-side value, and a signal merely relocates the same racy code.

questions.question: A local Docker stack has no AWS access. For a file-uploads feature the team weighs (a) a dev-only local-filesystem storage branch versus (b) running MinIO as a container. What is the main architectural argument for MinIO?
choices: MinIO's web console gives developers a UI to inspect uploaded files, which a bare filesystem cannot offer
MinIO persists objects across container restarts, while a local filesystem loses them
The S3 API handles large uploads faster than local filesystem I/O
The same boto3/S3 code path runs unchanged in every environment — only the endpoint differs — so no untested dev-only branch can drift from production
questions.explanation: A filesystem fallback is a code path that never executes in production, which is exactly how storage bugs hide until deploy: the branch quietly drifts because nothing exercises it against real behavior. MinIO serves the actual S3 API, so swapping it for AWS is a change of address, not of code — one path, exercised locally exactly as in prod. The console is a convenience, not the argument; a filesystem persists fine (MinIO also needs a mounted volume); and raw local I/O is generally faster than any HTTP API, so performance argues the other way.

questions.question: Bagging (Random Forests) trains deep trees in parallel on bootstrap samples and averages them; boosting trains small trees sequentially, each fit to the ensemble's remaining errors. Which failure mode does each architecture attack?
choices: Bagging reduces variance by cancelling uncorrelated errors across trees; boosting reduces bias by successively removing systematic error
Bagging reduces bias by averaging away systematic error; boosting reduces variance by keeping each tree small
Both reduce variance — boosting is simply a faster route to the same ensemble average
Bagging eliminates overfitting entirely; boosting trades bias for variance through the learning rate
questions.explanation: Averaging many independently-trained, unstable trees cancels noise that points in different directions — variance drops — but an error every tree shares survives the average (the ρσ² floor in Var(mean) = ρσ² + (1−ρ)σ²/k), so bias stays put. Boosting flips the architecture: each new tree studies what the running sum still gets wrong, shaving off systematic error round after round — which is also why gradient boosting can overfit and needs shrinkage and early stopping where a Random Forest mostly saturates. The two are complementary answers to the single-tree bias/variance trade-off, not interchangeable speedups.

questions.question: A service function decorated with @transaction.atomic saves an uploaded file to object storage, then creates the related DB rows; row creation raises. What must the except block do, and why?
choices: Nothing — atomic() rolls back the whole operation, including the stored file
Re-raise inside a nested atomic() block so a savepoint also covers the file write
Delete the stored blob explicitly before re-raising: storage writes are never enrolled in the DB transaction, so rollback alone leaves an orphaned file
Call save(update_fields=[]) on the document so no partially-written rows persist
questions.explanation: transaction.atomic rolls back rows, not side effects: a blob written to disk or object storage sits outside the database transaction, so on exception the rows vanish but the file survives as an orphan. The seam must track what it wrote and clean up itself — doc.file.delete(save=False), then re-raise so the DB rollback still happens. Nested atomic() blocks only add savepoints over DB state, and update_fields shapes a future write rather than undoing anything. This is also why validating everything before the first write is so valuable: a rejection then needs no cleanup at all.

questions.question: You train LightGBM with objective='quantile', alpha=0.95 for an SLA upper bound, then compare it against the default L2 model using RMSE — and the quantile model looks strictly worse. What is the methodological error?
choices: alpha=0.95 is too extreme for RMSE; re-comparing both models at alpha=0.5 would make the metric valid
RMSE is minimised by predicting the conditional mean, so it structurally favours the L2 model; a quantile model must be judged with pinball loss at its own alpha
The quantile model should have been trained with metric='rmse' so that training and evaluation objectives match
Nothing — the comparison is valid and shows the L2 model produces the better upper bound
questions.explanation: Choosing a loss chooses the statistic you estimate: squared error is minimised by the mean, pinball loss at alpha by that quantile. A model deliberately pushed up to the 95th percentile will always score worse on RMSE — that says nothing about how well it estimates the quantile, which is the thing the SLA needs. The right yardstick is pinball loss at the model's own alpha (metric='quantile' or mean_pinball_loss). Re-comparing at alpha=0.5 doesn't help — that targets the median, still not the mean — and setting an RMSE metric on a quantile objective just misreports training progress instead of fixing the mismatch.

questions.question: "As a QA engineer, I can have the test database restart automatically each night, so that I can test faster." Which trap is this, and what is the tell?
choices: Fake Story — the need sits inside the team's own Zone of Control; it is an internal task dressed in story costume
Misleading Story — the goal prescribes a technical solution while hiding the real underlying problem
Dependency-Locked — the deliverable depends on infrastructure outside the team's control
Micro-Story — it is sliced so thin it carries no standalone business risk
questions.explanation: A healthy story's need lives in the Sphere of Influence — user behaviour, business outcomes the team can affect but not control — while its deliverable sits in the Zone of Control. Here both the beneficiary and the need are inside the team: nothing about a user or the business is at stake, so there is no risk the story format exists to manage — the team is writing itself tickets. The honest fix is to track it as a Task, not to reject the work. A Misleading Story differs in that a genuine user need exists but is hidden behind a prescribed mechanism; here there is no external need at all.

questions.question: In Context-Anchored Specifications, every dictionary-sense use of a term in Stories and AC must be backticked. What design goal turns this rule from decoration into infrastructure?
choices: Backticks render terms in monospace, making specifications easier for stakeholders to scan
It lets a linter verify that code classes and endpoints are named after Dictionary terms
It prevents authors from using undefined terms, since a backtick requires an existing Dictionary entry
When a definition changes, grepping the backticked term mechanically yields exactly the artifacts that need re-review — the Dictionary becomes a navigable index of the spec
questions.explanation: Highlighting (Rule 1) exists to serve propagation (Rule 9): when a term's meaning shifts mid-iteration, the re-review set is precisely the Stories and AC whose text contains the backticked term — a mechanical grep, not a memory exercise. Without that propagation contract, the highlighting overhead buys nothing and backticks are decoration. Monospace rendering and format-survival are why backticks were chosen as the marker, not the goal; carrying terms into code is Rule 10, encouraged but never syntax-checked; and nothing enforces an entry exists at write time — unanchored artifacts are caught at the recurring review.

questions.question: You add a required column with AddField(default='TBD'). Django's generated SQL adds the column with a database default and then immediately drops that default. Why does this break a rolling deploy?
choices: Old code still running during the rollout inserts rows without naming the column; with the DB default gone, the NOT NULL column rejects those inserts — db_default persists the default and fixes it
The DROP DEFAULT statement takes an exclusive table lock that blocks all reads for the duration of the rollout window
New code reads the Python-side default while the database returns NULL, so the two code versions silently diverge on the value
The migration becomes irreversible once the default is dropped, so a failed deploy strands the schema mid-change
questions.explanation: A rolling deploy has a coexistence window where old and new code hit the same schema, and the old code's INSERT statements simply don't mention the new column — only a database-level default can fill it in. Django drops the DB default because it manages defaults in Python at the ORM layer, which is safe on a single-version system but exactly wrong mid-rollout. db_default (Django 5.0+) persists the default in the schema itself, so the DROP DEFAULT never happens and old-code inserts keep succeeding. Lock contention is a real but separate migration concern, and the failure here is rejected writes, not divergent reads or irreversibility.

questions.question: A host runs an app behind NGINX on 443 (TLS, auth, IP filtering) plus a management tool bound directly to 9443, and the Security Group allows 9443 from 0.0.0.0/0. What is the management tool's actual security posture?
choices: Protected — NGINX runs on the host, so it filters all HTTP traffic entering the instance regardless of port
Exposed to the entire internet — NGINX only governs ports it serves, so 9443 traffic reaches the tool directly with none of NGINX's controls applied
Protected — the Security Group is stateful, so unsolicited inbound connections are blocked automatically
Exposed only to clients that can complete a TLS handshake against the tool's own certificate
questions.explanation: The Security Group and NGINX are gates in series, and NGINX guards only the ports it actually listens on — a port the SG opens that NGINX doesn't proxy is an unguarded door straight to whatever is bound there. Traffic to 9443 never touches NGINX, so its TLS policy, auth, and allow/deny rules simply don't apply; and 'stateful' means allowed inbound connections get their return traffic automatically — it doesn't block connections the rules explicitly permit. TLS on the tool itself encrypts the channel but authenticates nobody. The fix is one of two moves: restrict 9443 at the SG, or route the tool through NGINX — never leave an open port pointing at an unguarded service.
